---
label: "Multi-Vector & Late Interaction"
icon: "🔍"
---

## Multi-Vector & Late Interaction

Standard bi-encoder embeddings compress an entire document into a single vector, losing fine-grained token-level information. Multi-vector approaches like **ColBERT** represent each document as a set of token-level vectors, enabling more precise matching through late interaction.

<ExpandableSection title="ColBERT: Late Interaction Retrieval" icon="🔍" defaultOpen={true}>

**ColBERT (Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT)** represents each token in the query and document as a separate embedding vector. At search time, it computes the maximum similarity between each query token and all document tokens (MaxSim), then sums these scores.

**MaxSim formula:** Score(Q, D) = sum over q_i of max over d_j of (q_i . d_j)

This captures token-level semantic matching that single-vector models miss. For example, a query about "Python memory management" would separately match "Python" tokens to programming context and "memory management" to systems concepts.

**Trade-off:** Much better retrieval quality than bi-encoders, but requires storing N vectors per document (where N is the token count). A 200-token document needs 200 x 128-dim vectors = 100KB vs 6KB for a single 1536-dim vector.

</ExpandableSection>

<CodeBlock b64="" />

<ExpandableSection title="When to Use Multi-Vector vs Single-Vector" icon="⚖️">

**Use single-vector (bi-encoder) when:**

- Storage is constrained (millions to billions of documents)
- Latency requirements are very tight (&lt;10ms)
- General semantic similarity is sufficient

**Use multi-vector (ColBERT) when:**

- Retrieval precision is critical (legal, medical, technical)
- Dataset is moderate size (&lt;10M documents)
- Queries contain multiple distinct concepts that need separate matching

</ExpandableSection>

<Quiz question="ColBERT stores 128-dimensional vectors for each token in a document. A corpus has 1 million documents averaging 200 tokens each. How much storage is needed for the token vectors alone (float32)?" options={["~10 GB","~50 GB","~97 GB","~500 GB"]} correctIndex={2} explanation="1M docs x 200 tokens x 128 dims x 4 bytes = 102.4 billion bytes = ~97 GB. This is ~16x more than a single-vector approach (1M x 1536 x 4 = 5.7 GB). The storage cost is the main trade-off for ColBERT's superior retrieval quality."/>

<Quiz question="What is the key advantage of late interaction (MaxSim) over early interaction (single-vector cosine similarity)?" options={["Late interaction is faster","Late interaction captures token-level semantic matching that single vectors compress away","Late interaction uses less memory","Late interaction works with any embedding model"]} correctIndex={1} explanation="Single-vector embeddings compress all token information into one vector, losing fine-grained matching capability. Late interaction preserves per-token representations and computes matching at the token level (MaxSim), capturing nuanced relationships that compression destroys."/>
